msgs.text:
Turn 49 — D.3 coordinate certificate audit: replace a fragile raw vector by a canonical solver certificate I re-read only the three files attached to this turn. The problem remains the over-G isomorphism question for the two one-relator quotients ΓS,ΓH defined in the attached problem statement. The proof sweep is now in the certificate phase: the structural conclusion is already ΓS≅GΓH, and the remaining issue is reproducibility of one finite witness, not a new obstruction search. The attached history explicitly records that after Turn 48 all nonlinear witness blocks are structurally accounted for and that the sole remaining item is the raw coordinate printout for one κ∈kerD5,6,7 whose coefficient-Bockstein shadow changes the d=4 square coordinates by (1,1,0,0,0,0).

The new point of this turn is:the request “print the raw κ coordinates” is basis-dependent and should be replaced by a canonical finite solver certificate.This is not a retreat from the D.3 certificate. It is the right reproducibility format. A raw coordinate vector has no intrinsic meaning until the following are all fixed:a concrete basis of L5,L6,L7;actual restricted group representatives for those basis vectors, not just primitive initial forms;an ordering of the three correction coordinates Y0,Y1,q;a convention for the degree 6,7,8 continuation-target basis;a convention for projecting the coefficient-Bockstein shadow back to the ordered L4[2]-basis.The attached history gives the structural D.3 facts, but it also explicitly says the raw κ-coordinates have not been printed in a chosen 3L5⊕3L6⊕3L7 basis.So inventing a coordinate vector here would be mathematically unsafe: the same κ has different coordinate strings under different valid basis choices.Certificate D.3′: canonical solver form The appendix certificate should define κ intrinsically as the lexicographically first solution of a finite linear system over F2, with all bases generated by the already specified primitive-basis procedure.Use the Turn 44 canonical primitive basis scheme: for each n, order words of length n lexicographically, build the reduced coproduct matrixΔn:Tn→i=1⨁n−1Ti⊗Tn−i,and defineLn=kerΔn.The history records this as the uniform certificate format for the primitive/rank backbone, including the secondary quotient ranks.

Then define the domain basisB5,6,7:=(3L5)⊕(3L6)⊕(3L7)by the ordered blocks(Y0L5,Y1L5,qL5;Y0L6,Y1L6,qL6;Y0L7,Y1L7,qL7),using the canonical bases of L5,L6,L7.

Define the continuation matrixD5,6,7:3L5⊕3L6⊕3L7⟶T6⊕T7⊕T8 as the finite linear map obtained by inserting actual restricted representative corrections of degrees 5,6,7 into the residual expressionS(Y0,Y1,q)=(Y1,Y1q)−1Y1−2Y03Y0q around the fixed I9 -base and retaining homogeneous degrees 6,7,8. The attached history gives the relevant numerical certificate:rankD5,6,7=60,dim(3L5⊕3L6⊕3L7)=105,hence dimkerD5,6,7=45.

Define the coefficient-Bockstein shadow mapB4:kerD5,6,7⟶L4[2]by extracting the induced change in the d=4 square-coordinate vector in the ordered basis(e0[2],e1[2],e2[2],e3[2],e4[2],e5[2]).D.3 then becomes the following finite certificate:

Find κ∈F2105 such that D5,6,7 κ=0,B4(κ)=(1,1,0,0,0,0).The history already proves this affine system is nonempty: the induced mapkerD5,6,7→H4(2)has rank 1, and a continuation-kernel direction changes the square coordinates by (1,1,0,0,0,0), turning the fixed value (1,0,1,1,1,0)into (0,1,1,1,1,0),so thatμ4=1+1=0.Thus the appendix should not merely print an arbitrary coordinate vector. It should print:κcan:=lexmin {z∈F2105:D5,6,7 z=0,B4 (z)=(1,1,0,0,0,0)}.That is a canonical coordinate certificate. Anyone using the same primitive-basis algorithm and representative-selection rules gets the same vector.

Why this is better than hammering one raw vector A single hard-coded κ is brittle. If the basis of L6 is changed by row reduction choices, or if the actual restricted representative basis is reordered, the coordinate vector changes while the certificate remains true. The invariant content is the existence of a kernel element with the specified Bockstein shadow, not the incidental coordinate string.This matches the structural lesson repeated throughout the sweep: fixed-gauge objects repeatedly looked like obstructions, while the full lower-plus-continuation torsor killed them. The D.3 scalar is the coefficient analogue of the residual degree-16 event: a fixed continuation gives μ4=1, but the continuation fiber moves it to μ4=0.

Consequence for the proof ledger After Turn 48, D.4 was explicit: (0,B8)∈ker([A,−]+[B,−]), andθ80(0,B8)=(B8)[2],with nonzero value under the residual missing functional, so the d=8 coefficient exceptional direction dies in the quotient.

So the nonlinear ledger now becomes:

Block	Status after Turn 49 D.1 residual degree-10 Bockstein witnesses	explicit witnesses printed D.2 residual degree-16 lower-base toggle	explicit structural witness printed D.3 A2,d=4,μ4 -toggle	structurally certified; canonical solver certificate defined D.4 coefficient d=8 quotient witness	explicit witness printed This is enough for the proof narrative. For a fully reproducible appendix, the next artifact should be a verifier table or script that constructs D5,6,7, constructs B4, row-reduces the augmented system, and prints κcan. The proof itself no longer depends on seeing the raw coordinate string in prose.

ESTABLISHED: The D.3 gap should be treated as a canonical finite linear certificate, not as an intrinsic hand-written vector. The invariant certificate is the nonempty affine systemD5,6,7 κ=0,B4 (κ)=(1,1,0,0,0,0),and the attached history already records the rank/effect proving this system has a solution.

GAP: The raw lexicographically first coordinate vector κcan has still not been printed. This is now a reproducibility artifact, not a mathematical obstruction.

NEXT: Produce the verifier certificate: implement the canonical primitive bases, construct D5,6,7 and B4, row-reduce the augmented system, and print κcan together with the checks D5,6,7κcan=0 and B4(κcan)=(1,1,0,0,0,0).[TURN49-D3-CANONICAL-SOLVER-CERTIFICATE]
